questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A metal surface having work function ' $\mathrm{w}_{0}{ }^{\prime}$ emits photoelectrons when photons of energy 'E' are incident on it. The electron enters the uniform magnetic field (B) in perpendicular direction and moves in circular path of radius 'r'. Then 'r' is equal to (m and e be the mass and charge of electron respectively).
questions.solution:
Kinetic energy of the electron $\mathrm{K}=\mathrm{E}-\omega_{0}$
Momentum $P=\sqrt{2 m k}=\sqrt{2 m\left(E-\omega_{\mathrm{o}}\right)}$
Radius of the circular path $r=\frac{P}{e B}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m\left(E-\omega_{0}\right)}}{e B}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A silver ball of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>4.8</mtext><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> is suspended by a thread in a vacuum chamber. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>UV</mi></math> light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>nm</mi></math> is incident on the ball for some time during which total energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> falls on the surface. Assuming that on an average, one out of <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> photons incident on the ball is able to eject an electron, then the potential of the sphere will be
questions.solution:
The total number of photons striking the surface of the ball is

np=Eλhc=1 × 10-7×200×10-96.6×10-34×3×108=1×1011

The total number of electrons ejected is

ne=1011103=108

∴V=q4πε0r=108×1.6×10-19×9×1094.8×10-2=3 V
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If $\lambda_0$ is the de-Broglie wavelength for a proton accelerated through a potential difference of $100 \mathrm{~V}$, the de-Broglie wavelength for $\alpha$-particle accelerated through the same potential difference is
questions.solution:
$$
V=100 \text { volt }
$$
Given, $\lambda_0=$ de-Broglie wavelength of proton
$$
\therefore \quad \lambda_0=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_p q_p V}}
$$
de-Broglie wavelength of $\alpha$-particle
$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda & =\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_\alpha q_\alpha V}} \\
m_\alpha & =4 m_p
\end{aligned}
$$
(Mass of $\alpha$-particle is four times of proton)
$$
q_\alpha=2 q_p
$$
(Charge of $\alpha$-particle is twice of proton)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad \lambda & =\frac{h}{\sqrt{2\left(4 m_p\right)\left(2 q_p\right) V}} \\
& =\frac{1}{\sqrt{8}} \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_p q_p V}}=\frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{8}}=\frac{\lambda_0}{2 \sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The work function of a certain metal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>h</mi><mi>c</mi></mrow><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mfrac></math>. When a monochromatic light of wavelength, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo><</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is incident such that the plate gains a total power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>. If the efficiency of photoelectric emission is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>η</mi><mo> </mo><mo>%</mo></math> and all the emitted photoelectrons are captured by a hollow conducting sphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, already charged to potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math>, then neglecting any interaction between the plate and the sphere, expression of potential of the sphere at a time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
The total energy of light incident=Pt.

Number of photons emitted in time

t=Energy incidentEnergy of 1 photon

Number of photons emitted in time

t=Pthcλ

If the efficiency is η %

So, number of photoelectrons emitted in time t, n=η %Ptλhc

Number of photoelectrons emitted in time t, n=ηPtλ100hc

These n number of photoelectrons having a total charge ηePtλ100hc are added to sphere, hence, its potential is given by,

Potential of the sphere after time t is,

V'=V-n4πεoR

V'=V-ηPλet400πε0Rhc
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The P.E. ' $U$ ' of a moving particle of mass ' $m$ ' varies with ' $\mathrm{x}$ ' is shown in the figure. The de-Broglie wavelength of the particle in the regions $0 \leq x \leq 1$ and $x>1$ are $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ respectively. If the total energy of the particle is ' $\mathrm{nE}$ ', then the ratio $\lambda_1 / \lambda_2$ is
questions.solution:
In the region $0 \leq x \leq 1$, the potential energy of the particle is $E$. Total energy is $\mathrm{nE}$.
Hence, kinetic energy, $\mathrm{K}=\mathrm{nE}-\mathrm{E}=(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{E}$
Its momentum, $\mathrm{p}_1=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{E}}$
$$
\therefore \lambda_1=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{p}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{E}}}
$$
In the region $\mathrm{x}>1, \mathrm{PE}$ is zero
Hence, total is kinetic energy.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \mathrm{K}=\mathrm{nE} \\
& \therefore \lambda_2=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{p}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mnE}}} \\
& \therefore \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n}-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The maximum kinetic energy of a photoelectron liberated from the surface of lithium with work function $2.35 \mathrm{eV}$ by electromagnetic radiation whose electric component varies with time as :
$E=a\left[1+\cos \left(2 \pi f_1 t\right)\right] \cos 2 \pi f_2 t$ (where $a$ is a constant $)$ is $\left(f_1=3.6 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{~Hz}\right.$,
and $f_2=1.2 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{~Hz}$ and Planck's constant $\left.h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right)$
questions.solution:
Here, work function, $W_0=2.35 \mathrm{eV}$ and the electric component of electromagnetic radiation
$$
\begin{aligned}
& E=a\left[1+\cos \left(2 \pi f_1 t\right)\right] \cos \left(2 \pi f_2 t\right) \\
& \Rightarrow E=\left[a \cos \left(2 \pi f_2 t\right)+a \cos \left(2 \pi f_1 t\right) \cos \left(2 \pi f_2 t\right)\right] \\
& \left(\because \cos A \cos B=\frac{1}{2}[\cos (A+B)-\cos (A-B))\right. \\
& \Rightarrow E=a \cos \left(2 \pi f_2 t\right)+\frac{a}{2} \cos 2 \pi\left(f_1+f_2\right) t-\frac{a}{2} \cos 2 \pi\left(f_1-f_2\right) t
\end{aligned}
$$

So, the electric component has 3 sub-components with frequencies are,
$$
f_2,\left(f_1+f_2\right) \text { and }\left(f_1-f_2\right)
$$

So, for maximum kinetic energy of photoelectron, we take photon of maximum frequency. Hence,
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\max } & =\frac{h v_{\max }}{e}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times\left(3.6 \times 10^{15}+1.2 \times 10^{15}\right)}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\
& =19.8 \mathrm{eV}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the maximum kinetic energy,
$$
\mathrm{KE}_{\text {max }}=E_{\text {max }}-W_0=19.8-2.35=17.45 \mathrm{eV}
$$

Hence, the correct option is (d).
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Photoelectric emission is observed from a metallic surface for frequencies $v_1$ and $v_2$ of the incident light rays $\left(v_1>v_2\right)$. If the maximum values of kinetic energy of the photoelectrons emitted in the two cases are in the ratio of $1: k$, then the threshold frequency of the metallic surface is
questions.solution:
Maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons
$(\mathrm{KE})_{\max }=h v-h v_0$
$v=$ frequency of incident radiation
$v_0=$ threshold frequency
Ist Case $\quad\left(\mathrm{KE}_{\max }\right)_1=h v_1-h v_0$
IInd Case $\left(\mathrm{KF}_{\max }\right)_2=h v_2-h v_0$
$\begin{aligned} \frac{\left(\mathrm{KE}_{\max }\right)_1}{\left(\mathrm{KE}_{\max }\right)_2} & =\frac{h\left(v_1-v_0\right)}{h\left(v_2-v_0\right)} \\ \frac{1}{k} & =\frac{v_1-v_0}{v_2-v_0} \\ v_2-v_0 & =k v_1-k v_0 \\ k v_0-v_0 & =k v_1-v_2 \\ v_0 & =\frac{k v_1-v_2}{k-1}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A charged particle is accelerated from rest through a certain potential difference. The de-Broglie wavelength is $\lambda_1$ when it is accelerated through $V_1$ and is $\lambda_2$ when accelerated through $V_2$. The ratio $\lambda_1 / \lambda_2$ is
questions.solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength associated with a charged particle is given by
$$
\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{m q V}}
$$
where, $h=$ planck's constant
$m=$ mass of charged particle
$q=$ charge on the particle
$V=$ potential at which the charged particle is accelerated.
$\therefore$
$$
\lambda_1=\frac{h}{\sqrt{m_1 q V_1}}, \lambda_2=\frac{h}{\sqrt{m_2 q V_2}}
$$
Here,
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_1 & =m_2 \\
\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} & =\sqrt{\frac{m_2 q V_2}{m_1 q V_1}}=\sqrt{\frac{V_2}{V_1}} \\
& =V_2^{\frac{1}{2}} \cdot V_1^{\frac{1}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Photoelectric effect supports quantum nature of light because,

1. There is a minimum frequency of light below which, no photoelectrons are emitted.

2. Electric charge of photoelectrons is quantized.

3. Maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons depends only on the frequency of light and not on its intensity.

4. Even when metal surface is faintly illuminated, the photoelectrons leave the surface immediately.
questions.solution:
Photoelectric effect supports quantum nature of light because,

1. There is a minimum frequency of light below which, no photoelectrons are emitted.

2. Maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons depends only on the frequency of light and not on its intensity.

3. Even when metal surface is faintly illuminated. the photoelectrons leave the surface immediately.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two particles <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> of same mass have their de-Broglie wavelengths in the ratio <math><msub><mrow><mi>X</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mo>:</mo><msub><mrow><mi>X</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>K</mi><mi> </mi><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Their potential energies <math><msub><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mo>:</mo><msub><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mo>:</mo><msup><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The ratio of their total energies <math><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mo>:</mo><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></math> is
questions.solution:
According to question, XAXB=K1 ...(i)

UAUB=1K2

So, UA=y and UB=K2y

According to de-Broglie wavelength,

λ=h2mK1 .....(ii)

Here, K1 is kinetic energy.

So, from Equations (i) and (ii),

⇒  h2mK1Ah2mK1B=K1⇒K1BK1A=K1

⇒  K1BK1A=K2

So, K1B=K2x and K1A=x

∵  Total energy, E=K1+U

So, EA=K1A+UA

and EB=K1B+UB

or EAEB=K1A+UAK1B+UB=x+yK2x+K2y

⇒ So, EAEB=1K2 or EA :EB=1 :K2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When certain metal surface is illuminated with a light of wavelength <math><mi>λ</mi></math> , the stopping potential is V, when the same surface is illuminated by light of wavelength <math><mn>2</mn><mi>λ</mi></math> , the stopping potential is <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> . The threshold wavelength for the surface is
questions.solution:
Given for a metal, wavelength of light used = λ stopping potential =V
If λ0 be the threshold wavelength, then maximum kinetic energy of emitted electrons
Kmax=hc1λ-1λ0 …(i)
Again, wavelength of used light
λ'=2λ
Stopping potential, V’ =V3
then Kmax=hc1λ,1λ0
⇒eV'hc12λ-1λ0
⇒eV3=hc12λ,1λ0 …. (ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have
eVeV3=hc1λ-1λ0hc12λ,1λ0
312λ-1λ0=1λ-1λ0
⇒λ0=4λ
So, threshold wavelength is 4 times of wavelength of ligh.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> and intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> falls on the surface of a photosensitive material. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the incident photons produce photo electron, the number of photoelectrons emitted from an area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> of the surface is nearly
questions.solution:
Power of radiation is expressed as, Power=Nhcλ

Here, N is the number of photons per unit time per unit area.

⇒P=Nhcλ⇒N=Pλhc=100 W m-2×300×10-9 m6.6×10-34 J s×3×108 m s-1=1.51×1020 m-2 s-1

Given that 2% of the incident, photons produce photoelectron therefore, number of photoelectrons produced by photons is =2100×1.51×1020 m-2 s-1

Now, the number of photoelectrons emitted from an area of 2 cm2 of the surface in 1 s is nearly 

n=1 s2×10-4 m2×3.02×1018 m-2 s-1n=6.04×1014
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Photo-electrons are produced by from a metal surface using a radiation of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6561</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>∘</mo></mover></math>. These photo-electrons, when made to enter a uniform magnetic field of intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>, move along different circular paths with a maximum radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, then the work function of the metal is close to
questions.solution:
KEmax=E-ϕ

=12400λ( in A)-ϕ  ( in eV )

∴r=2mKEeB

KEmax=r2e2B22m

=r2eB22m (in J)

∴ ϕ=124006561-r2eB22m

=1.1 eV
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: An electron of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> and magnitude of charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math> at rest, gets accelerated by a constant electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>. The rate of change of de-Broglie wavelength of this electron at a time <math><mi>t</mi></math> is (ignore relativistic effects)
questions.solution:
The acceleration of the electron is

a=eEm

The velocity of the electron as a function of time is

v=at=eEmt

The de-Broglie wavelength is

λ=hmv

λ=hmeEmt

λ=heEt
dλdt=-heEt2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two electrons in two hydrogen-like atoms <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> have their total energies <math><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></math> in the ratio <math><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math> . Their potential energies <math><msub><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></math> are in the ratio <math><msub><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mo>:</mo><msub><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math> . If <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></math> are their de-Broglie wavelengths, then <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mo>:</mo><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></math> is
questions.solution:
Given,

EAEB=12,UAUB=12

So EA=x, EB=2x

And UA=y, UB=2y

∵  EA=UA+KA

And EB=UB+KB

here KA and KB are kinetic energy of particles A and B

So KA=EA-UA=x-y  .....(i)

KB=EB-UB=2x-y ....(ii)

∵ de-Broglie wavelength,

λ=h2mk

So λA=h2mKA, λB=h2mKB

∴   λAλB=KBKA .....(iii)

From Equation (i), (ii) and (iii),

λAλB=2x-yx-y=21
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When light of wavelength $\lambda$ is incident on a photosensitive surface the stopping potential is ' $\mathrm{V}$ '. When light of wavelength $3 \lambda$ is incident on same surface the stopping potential is $\frac{\mathrm{V}}{6}$. Then the threshold wavelength for the surface is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \lambda_1=\lambda,\left(\mathrm{V}_0\right)_1=\mathrm{V} \\
& \lambda_2=3 \lambda,\left(\mathrm{V}_0\right)_2=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$
Photo electric equation is given by $\mathrm{eV}_0=\mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_0}\right)....(i)$
In first case,
$$
\mathrm{eV}=\mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_0}\right)
$$
For second case,
$$
\frac{\mathrm{eV}}{6}=\mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{1}{3 \lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_0}\right).....(ii)
$$
Dividing equation (i) by equation (ii),
$$
\begin{aligned}
6 & =\frac{\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_0}}{\frac{1}{3 \lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_0}}=\frac{3 \lambda_0-\lambda}{\lambda_0-3 \lambda} \\
\therefore \quad \lambda_0 & =5 \lambda
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The threshold wavelength of a photosensitive material is equal to the frequency of $\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{a}}$ line of hydrogen. If a photon whose frequency equal to the frequency of $\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{b}}$ line of hydrogen is incident on this photosensitive material, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is (R-Rydberg's constant, h- Flanck's constant and c- speed of light in vacuum)
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A photon released by the transition of an electron from the second excited state to the ground state of Hydrogen atom is incident on the surface of a metal of work function $3.1 \mathrm{eV}$. The de Broglie wavelength of the most energetic electron emitted from that metal surface is nearly
questions.solution:
Work function, $\mathrm{W}=3.1 \mathrm{eV}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { hu }=13.6\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right] \\
& =13.6\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]=13.6\left[1-\frac{1}{9}\right]=13.6 \times \frac{8}{9} \\
& =12.1 \mathrm{eV} .
\end{aligned}
$$
From Einstein's photoelectric equation
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{hv}=\mathrm{K}_{\max }+\mathrm{W} \\
& \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\mathrm{hv}-\mathrm{w} \\
& =12.1-3.1 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=9 \mathrm{eV} \\
& \lambda=\frac{66 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{9 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=1.3 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A perfectly reflecting solid hemisphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is placed in the path of a parallel beam of light of large aperture. If the beam carries an intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>, find the force exerted by the beam on the hemisphere.
questions.solution:
Let O be the centre of the sphere and OZ be the line possible to the incident beam (diagram). Consider a radius OP of the sphere making an angle θ with OZ. Rotate this radius about OZ to get another circle on the sphere. Change θ to θ+dθ and rotate the radius about OZ to get another circle on the sphere. The part of the sphere between these circles is a ring of area 2πr2sinθdθ. Consider a small part of ΔA of this ring atP. The energy of the light falling on this part in time Δt is,

ΔU=IΔtΔAcosθ

The momentum of this light falling on ΔA is ΔUc along QP. The light is reflected by the sphere along PR. The change in the momentum, along the inward normal, is,

Δp=2ΔUccosθ=2cIΔtΔAcos2θ

The force on ΔA due to the light falling on it is,

ΔpΔt=2cIΔAcos2θ

The force is along PO. The resultant force on the ring as well as on the sphere is along ZO, by symmetry. The component of the force on ΔA along ZO is,

ΔpΔtcosθ=2cIΔAcos2θ

The force acting on the ring is,

dF=2cI2πr2sinθdθcos3θ

The force on the entire sphere is,

F=∫0π/24πr2Iccos3θsinθdθ

=-∫0π/24πr2Iccos3θdcosθ

=-∫θ=0π/24πr2Iccos4θ40π/2=πr2Ic

Note that integration is done only for the hemisphere, that faces the incident beam.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A photon of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> is absorbed by an electron confined to a box of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mn>35</mn><mi>h</mi><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>8</mn><mi>m</mi><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfrac></msqrt></math>. As a result, the electron makes a transition from state, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> to the state <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math>. Subsequently, the electron transits from the state <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> to the state <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> by emitting a photon of wavelength, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>λ</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mi>λ</mi></math>. Then,
questions.solution:
Let the length of confined box be, l=35hλ8mc.

Kinetic energy of electron after absorbing photon, K=hcλ.

The de-Broglie wavelength of electron with this kinetic energy is given as, λde Broglie=h2mK=h2mhcλ=hλ2mc.

Now, number of loops formed in the confined length is given as,

p=lλde Broglie2=2lλde Broglie⇒ p=2×35hλ8mchλ2mc=35≈6

Electron forms n loops in nth orbit. Hence, the required number of states of the electron after absorbing photon is n=6. This is present only in one option.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Statement (I) : By increasing the potential difference between cathode and anode continuously in a photoelectric experiment, the photo current always increases continuously.
Statement (II) : If two photons A and B of energies 2.5 $\mathrm{eV}$ and $3.5 \mathrm{eV}$ respectively fall on a metal surface of work function $2.0 \mathrm{eV}$, then the ratio of maximum kinetic energies emitted between A and B is 3 .
Statement (III) : The maximum energy needed by an electron to come out from metal surface is called the work function of the metal.
Which of the following is correct?
options: Statements I, II and III are true
Statements I, II are true, but statement III is false
Statements II, III are true, but statement I is false
Statements I, II and III are false
questions.solution:
Photocurrent will become constant after a certain potential difference between cathode and anode.
So, (I) is incorrect
Now,
$$
\frac{(\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E})_{\mathrm{A}}}{(\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E})_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{2.5-2}{3.5-2}=\frac{0.5}{1.5}=\frac{1}{3}
$$
So, (II) is incorrect
Work function is minimum energy needed to eject the electron.
So, (III) is in correct.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Graph shows the variation of de-Broglie wavelength $\lambda$ versus $\frac{1}{\sqrt{V}}$ where $V$ is the accelerating potential for four particles, P, Q, R, S carrying same charge but of mass $m_1, m_2, m_3, m_4$. Which one represents a particle of smaller mass?
questions.solution:
De-broglie wavelength,
$\lambda=\frac{h}{p}$
Kinetic energy :
$\begin{aligned}
& K=\frac{p^2}{2 m}=q V \\
& \lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}
\end{aligned}$
Slope of straight line
$\tan \theta=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q}}$
Hence, more is slope of line, less is the mass.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In a photoelectric effect experiment, stopping potential changes by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>30 Volt</mtext></math> if we change frequency of the radiation. The magnitude of change in the frequency is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mtext>h</mtext><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mtext>J s</mtext><mo>)</mo></math>
questions.solution:
eV 0 = h υ  -  h υ 0

V 0 = h e υ - h e υ 0

Slope m = h e = Δ V 0 Δ υ

   

Δ υ = e h × Δ V ρ

= 1.6 × 1 0 - 1 9 6 × 1 0 - 3 4 × 3 0 = 8 × 1 0 1 5  s - 1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The electric field associated with a light wave is given by <math><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1.57</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></math> (where <math><mi>x</mi></math> and <math><mi>t</mi></math> are in metre and second respectively). The stopping potential when this light is used in an experiment on photoelectric effect with the emitter having work function <math><mi>ϕ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1.9</mn><mi> </mi><mn>e</mn><mn>V</mn></math> is.
questions.solution:
Given, E=E0sin⁡1.57×107x-ct ...(i)

∴  E=E0sin⁡ωcx-ct ...(ii)

On comparing Equations (i) and (ii), we get

ωc=1.57×107

=π2×107

λ=4×10-7 m

=400 nm

As, ϕ0=hcλ-EK

1.9=1242400-EK

EK=3.1-1.9 eV=1.2 eV
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When radiation of wavelength ' $\lambda$ ' is incident on a metallic surface, the stopping potential is $4.8 \mathrm{~V}$. If the surface is illuminated with radiation of double the wavelength then the stopping potential becomes $1.6 \mathrm{~V}$.' The threshold wavelength for the surface is
questions.solution:
Stopping potential:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{eV}_0=\mathrm{h} v-\phi_0 \\
& \mathrm{eV}_0=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi_0
\end{aligned}$
For first case: $\mathrm{e}(4.8)=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi_0... (i)$
For Second case: $e(1.6)=\frac{h c}{2 \lambda}-\phi_0... (ii)$
Dividing equation (i) by equation (ii),
$\begin{aligned}
& 3\left(\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{2 \lambda}-\phi_0\right)=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi_0 \\
\therefore \quad & \frac{3 \mathrm{hc}}{2 \lambda}-3 \phi_0=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi_0 \\
& \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{2 \lambda}=2 \phi_0 \\
& \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{4 \lambda}=\phi_0
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad$ Threshold wavelength is $4 \lambda$.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and initial velocity $u$ collide with another particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>m</mi></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> which is at rest, The collision is head on and perfectly elastic. The ratio of de-Broglie wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>Y</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>X</mi></msub></math> after the collision is
questions.solution:
For perfectly elastic collision,

total momentum before collision = total momentum after collision

i.e. m·u+3m4×0=mvx+3m4·vy

⇒4u=4vx+3vy   ...i

Coefficient of restitution,

e=Velocity of seperation vy-vx after collision Velocity of approachux-uy before collision 

⇒1=vy-vxu-0   [∵ ux=v and uy=0]

u=vy-vx   ...ii

Solving Equations. (i) and (ii), we have

vx=u7 and uy=8u7

∴ λyλx=h/mvyh/3m4·vx=34·vxvy=34·u/78u/7=332
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In a photoelectric effect experiment the cathode metal is exposed to light of wavelength $600 \mathrm{~nm}$. The maximum kinetic energy of the ejected electron doubles when light of wavelength $400 \mathrm{~nm}$ is used. The work function of the cathode metal is approximately. [Use $h=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}-\mathrm{s}, c=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ ]
questions.solution:
Wavelength of light, $\lambda_1=600 \mathrm{~nm}$ If the work-function is $\phi$ and the kinetic energy of electron is $E_K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2$, then we can write,
Also, when the wavelength, $\lambda_2=400 \mathrm{~nm}$, Kinetic energy of electron is $2 E_K=m v^2$
Multiplying Eq. (i) by 2 , we get
By subtracting Eq (iii) from Eq. (ii), we get $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{h c}{\lambda_2}-\frac{2 h c}{\lambda_1}=m v^2+\phi-m v^2-2 \phi \\ & \phi=h c\left(\frac{2}{\lambda_1}-\frac{1}{\lambda_2}\right) \\ &=6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8\left(\frac{2}{600}-\frac{1}{400}\right) \times 10^9 \\ &=6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8\left(\frac{1}{300}-\frac{1}{400}\right) \times 10^9 \\ & \phi=1.02 \mathrm{eV} \end{aligned} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In a photoelectric experiment, a monochromatic light is incident on the emitter plate \(E\), as shown in the figure. When switch \(S_1\) is closed and switch \(S_2\) is open, the photoelectrons strike the collector plate \(C\) with a maximum kinetic energy of \(1 \mathrm{eV}\). If switch \(S_1\) is open and switch \(S_2\) is closed and the frequency of the incident light is doubled the photoelectrons strike the collector plate with a maximum kinetic energy of \(20 \mathrm{eV}\). The threshold wavelength of the emitter plate is
questions.solution:
Let threshold frequency of emitter plate \(=v_0\)
Energy of photon in first case is \(\mathrm{E}\).
When switch \(S_1\) is closed and switch \(S_2\) is open,
So, \(E=h v_0+(5+1) \mathrm{eV}\) ...(i)
For second case, when switch \(S_1\) is open and switch \(S_2\) is closed and frequency of incident light is doubled.
then, \(\quad 2 E=h v_0+(20-5) \mathrm{eV}\) ...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad 2\left(h v_0+6 \mathrm{eV}\right)=h v_0+15 \mathrm{eV} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 2 h v_0+12 \mathrm{eV}=h v_0+15 \mathrm{eV} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad h v_0=3 \mathrm{eV} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad v_0=\frac{3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}=7.25 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz} \\
& \lambda_0=\frac{c}{v_0}=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{7.25 \times 10^{14}} \\
& \lambda_0=41333 Å \\
\end{aligned}\)
Hence, the threshold wavelength of the emitter plate is \(4133.3 Å\).
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Photoelectrons are emitted with maximum velocity $\mathrm{v}$ when light of frequency $3 \mathrm{f}$ incidents on a photosensitive material of work function $2 \mathrm{hf}$. If the frequency of the incident light is $4.25 \mathrm{f}$, the maximum velocity of the emitted photoelectrons is
(h-Plank's constant)
questions.solution:
Initial frequency, $f_1=3 f$
Initial velocity $\mathrm{v}_1=\mathrm{v}$
Work function $\phi=2 \mathrm{hf}$
final frequency, $\mathrm{f}_2=4.25 \mathrm{f}$
final velocity, $\mathrm{v}_2=$ ?
Kinetic energy is given as,
$K E=h f-\phi$
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_1^2=3 \mathrm{hf}-2 \mathrm{hf}=\mathrm{hf}$
$m=\frac{2 h f}{v^2}$
final kinetic energy,
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2=4.25 \mathrm{hf}-2 \mathrm{hf} \\ & =2.25 \mathrm{hf}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 h f}{v^2} \times v_2^2=\frac{225}{100} \\ & \frac{v_2^2}{v^2}=\frac{225}{100} \\ & v_2^2=\frac{225}{100} v^2=\left(\frac{15}{10} v\right)^2 \\ & v_2=1.5 v\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Following graph shown the variation of stopping potential corresponding to the frequency of incident radiation $(v)$ for a given metal. The correct variation is shown in graph [ $v_0=$ threshold frequency]
questions.solution:
Concept: Stopping potential $V$ is related to the maximum kinetic energy $K_{\max }$ of the photoelectrons, via Einstien's photo-electric effect relation:
$e V=K_{\max }=h v-h v_0$
Therefore,
$V=\left(\frac{h}{e}\right) v-\left(\frac{h}{e}\right) v_0$
Therefore, $V$ vs $v$ is straighline with positive slope and at $v=v_0$ the stopping potential is zero.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If a photocell is illuminated with a radiation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1240</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>∘</mo></mover></math>, the stopping potential is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>; then the work function of the emitter and the threshold wavelength are:
questions.solution:
The Einstein's equation for photoelectric effect : KEmax=hcλ-W where,

W= work function of metal, λ= wavelength of incident light.

If V0 be the stopping potential, then KEmax=eV0

So, eV0=hcλ-W

W=hcλ-eV0=6.62×10-34×3×1081240×10-10-1.6×10-198
=3.2×10-19=2 eV as 1 eV=1.6×10-19

If λ0 be the threshold wavelength, W=hcλ0

λ0=hcW=6.62×10-34×3×1083.2×10-19=6.2×10-7 m=6200 A°
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Light of two different frequencies whose photons have energies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>eV</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>eV</mi></math>, respectively, successively illuminate a metallic surface whose work function is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>eV</mi></math>. The ratio of maximum speeds of the emitted electrons will be
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons is Kmax=hν-ϕ0

Where hν is the energy of the incident photon and ϕ0 is the work function.

But Kmax=12mvmax2

∴12mvmax2=hν-ϕ0

As per the question,

12mv2max1⁡=1 eV-0.5 eV=0.5 eV ...(i)

and 12mv2max2⁡=2.5 eV-0.5 eV=2 eV ...(ii)

Dividing equation (i) by equation (ii), we get

v2max1⁡v2max2⁡=0.5 eV2 eV=14

vmax1⁡vmax2⁡=14=12
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The potential energy of a particle varies as

Ux=E0 for 0≤x≤1
        =0 for x>1

For  0≤x≤1 , the de-Broglie wavelength is λ1  and for x>1  the de-Broglie wavelength is λ2. The total energy of the particle is  2E0. Find λ 1 λ 2
questions.solution:
For 0≤x≤1 , U=E0

∴ Kinetic energy, K1 = Total energy - U

=2E0-E0=E0
∴λ1=h2mE0

For x>1, U=0

∴ Kinetic energy K2 = Total energy -U

                                   =2E0

∴ λ 2 = h⁡ 2 m⁡ ( 2E⁡ 0 ) ...(ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have

                           λ 1 λ 2 = 2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: According to the photoelectric effect, the plot of kinetic energy of the emitted photo-electrons from a metal versus the frequency of the incident radiation gives a straight line whose slope
options: is the same for all metals and independent of the intensity of radiation
depends only on the intensity of radiation
depends both on the intensity of the radiation and the metal used
depends on the nature of metals used
questions.solution:
For photoelectric effect, $\phi=\phi_0+(\mathrm{KE})_{\max }$ where, $\phi=$ energy of incident photon $=h v$ $\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} & \phi_0= \text { work function of material of metal on } \\ & \text { which photon is falling } \\ &(\mathrm{KE})_{\max }= \text { Maximum kinetic energy of emitted } \\ & \text { photoelectrons. }\end{aligned} \\ & \text { So, on putting the values } \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} h v=\phi_0+(\mathrm{KE})_{\max } \\ \text { where, } v=\text { frequency of falling photon } \\ \text { and } h=\text { Planck's constant. }\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $m=h=$ constant So, the slope of $(\mathrm{KE})_{\max }$ versus $v$ graph is same for all metals and independent of the intensity of radiation.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A photocell is illuminated by a point source of light, which is placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> from the cell. If the distance becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>d</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, then number of electrons emitted per second will
questions.solution:
The number of electrons emitted per second is known as the intensity of the light. Intensity is given by, I=E4πr2t, where E is the energy of the source, r is the distance and t is the time.

We can see that the intensity of the incident light is inversely proportional to the square of the distance.

When r=d, I1=E4πd2t.

When r=d2, I2=E4πd22t.

We have I2=4I1. Therefore, intensity becomes four times which means the number of electrons will become four times.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The potential energy of a particle varies as

Ux=E0 for 0≤x≤1

        =0 for x>1

For  0≤x≤1 , the de-Broglie wavelength is λ1  and for x>1  the de-Broglie wavelength is λ2. The total energy of the particle is  2E0. Find λ 1 λ 2
questions.solution:
For 0≤x≤1 , U=E0

∴ Kinetic energy, K1 = Total energy - U

=2E0-E0=E0
∴λ1=h2mE0

For x>1, U=0

∴ Kinetic energy K2 = Total energy -U

                                   =2E0

∴ λ 2 = h⁡ 2 m⁡ ( 2E⁡ 0 ) ...(ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have

                           λ 1 λ 2 = 2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In a photoelectric experiment, a graph of maximum kinetic energy $\left(\mathrm{KE}_{\max }\right)$ against the frequency of incident radiation $(v)$ is plotted. If $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are the intercepts on the $\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y}$ axis respectively the Planck's constant is given by
questions.solution: Kinetic energy is given by $\text { (K.E.) })_{\max }=h v-h v_0$ $\text { Comparing }\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}+\mathrm{c}$ We get $\mathrm{x}$-intercept when $\text { (K.E. })_{\max }=0 \text {, i.e., } \mathrm{h} v-\mathrm{h} v_0=0$ Or $v=v_0=\mathrm{A}$ We get y-intercept when $v=0$ $\therefore(\text { K.E. })_{\max }=-\mathrm{h} v_0=\mathrm{B}$ $\therefore\left|\frac{\mathrm{B}}{\mathrm{A}}\right|=\frac{\mathrm{h} \mathrm{v}_0}{\mathrm{v}_0}=\mathrm{h}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: All electrons ejected from a metallic surface by incident light of wavelength \(400 \mathrm{~nm}\) travelled \(1 \mathrm{~m}\) in the direction of uniform electric field of \(2 \mathrm{NC}^{-1}\) and came to rest. The work function of the surface is
questions.solution:
Given, wavelength of incident radiation,
\(\lambda=400 \mathrm{~nm}=4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}\)
electric field, \(E=2 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}\) and distance, \(s=1 \mathrm{~m}\)
\(\therefore\) Energy of the incident light,
\(\begin{aligned}
E & =\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^{-7}} \\
& =4.95 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}=\frac{4.95 \times 10^{19}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\
\mathrm{~J} & =3.09 \simeq 3 \mathrm{leV}
\end{aligned}\)
If \(a\) be the retardation of emitted electrons in the electric field,
then, \(\quad a=\frac{q E}{m}\)
initial speed \(u\) of emitted electron is calculated as
\(v^2=u^2-2 a s \Rightarrow 0=u^2-2 a \times 1 \Rightarrow u^2=2 a=\frac{2 q E}{m}\)
\(\therefore\) Maximum kinetic energy of the electron,
\(\begin{aligned}
& K_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} m u^2=m \cdot \frac{2 q E}{m}=q E=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \\
& K_{\max }=3.2 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}=2 \mathrm{eV}
\end{aligned}\)
\(\therefore\) Work function of the surface,
\(W_0=E-K_{\max }=3.1-2=1.1 \mathrm{eV}\)
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When the energy of incident radiation is increased by $20 \%$, the kinetic energy of the photoelectrons emitted from a metal surface increased from $0.5 \mathrm{eV}$ to $0.8 \mathrm{eV}$. The work function of the metal is
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation,
$E_K=E-\phi_0$
where, $E_K=$ kinetic energy,
$E=$ energy of incident photon
and $\quad \phi_0=$ work function of metal surface.
$\Rightarrow \quad E=E_K+\phi_0$ $\ldots$ (i)
When energy of incident photon is increased by $20 \%$, then
$E^{\prime}=E+20 \% \text { of } E=E+\frac{20}{100} E=\frac{6 E}{5}$
$\Rightarrow \quad E^{\prime}=1.2 E$
$\therefore \quad E_K^{\prime}=E^{\prime}-\phi_0 \Rightarrow E^{\prime}=E_K^{\prime}+\phi_0$
$1.2 E=E_K^{\prime}+\phi_0$ $\ldots$ (ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$\frac{E}{1.2 E}=\frac{E_K+\phi_0}{E_K^{\prime}+\phi_0}=\frac{0.5+\phi_0}{0.8+\phi_0}$
$\left[\because E_K=0.5 \mathrm{eV}\right.$ and $E_K^{\prime}=0.8 \mathrm{eV}]$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{5}{6}=\frac{0.5+\phi_0}{0.8+\phi_0}$
$4+5 \phi_0=3+6 \phi_0 \Rightarrow \phi_0=1 \mathrm{eV}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In the following arrangement, y=1.0 mm, d=0.24 mm and D=1.2 m. The work function of the material of the emitter is 2.2 eV. The stopping potential V' needed to stop the photocurrent will be:
questions.solution:
In Young's double slit experiment,

Fringe Width, β=λDd

Distance between dark and bright fringe=y.

So,

β=2y

λDd=2y

λ=2×1×10-3×0.24×10-31.2 Ao

λ=4000 Ao

Energy of photon light incident, 

E=12400λAo

E(in eV)=124004000

E=3.1 eV

To calculate the stopping potential V'
applying Einstein's photoelectric equation,

E(eV)=W0+eV'

⇒3.1 eV=2.2 eV+eV'

⇒V'=0.9 V
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
